Content:
You gave your client your personal cell number.

Now they text you at 9 p.m. on a Sunday about business.

And you answer. Every single time. Because you think that's what separates you from the competition.

The very thing that "sets you apart" in the short term may become your downfall.

24/7 availability doesn't make you a better Amazon seller or a better business owner. It makes you a single point of failure with no off switch.

Your customers don't need you at midnight. 

They need their problem solved by someone competent within a reasonable window. 

That person doesn't even have to be you.

Set the hours. Communicate them clearly. And then actually honor them (because if you don't respect your own boundaries, nobody else will either).

The best thing I ever did for my clients was stop being available to all of them all the time. 

My responses got sharper. 
My thinking got clearer. 

And not a single person left because I answered at 9 a.m. instead of 11 p.m.

Protect your time like it's your best-selling product. Because it is.

Content:
You're paying for ads on Amazon. 

Your competitors are paying for ads on your name.

If you're not actively bidding on your own branded keywords, someone else is. And they're siphoning conversions from shoppers who were already looking for you.

This happens constantly. A customer types your brand name into Amazon search, and the first thing they see is a Sponsored Brands banner from a competitor with a similar product and a lower price point. That shopper was yours. Now they're comparison shopping.

Most sellers don't even realize this is happening because they assume branded traffic is safe. It's not. On Amazon, your brand name is just another keyword. Anyone can bid on it.

You need a Sponsored Brands presence on your own name. Your storefront needs to be optimized for branded traffic. And you need to be monitoring those placements regularly, because competitors rotate in and out.

You spent months (maybe years) building recognition. Letting someone else rent your name out from under you for a few cents per click is one of the most expensive mistakes you can make on the platform.

Own your name. 

Or someone else will use it to sell against you.

Content:
Fake Amazon reviews used to be lazy.

Hire a VA
Hand them a script
Copy and paste the same negative review across listings. 

It was sloppy. It was obvious. And honestly, it was pretty easy to catch.

That era is over.

We're now seeing fake reviews that read like real customer horror stories. Specific details. Emotional language. The kind of thing that makes a potential buyer stop scrolling and think twice.

Pet supplement gets hit with a review about a dog having an emergency vet visit. Detailed symptoms. Named breeds. Timeframes for when the reaction happened.

None of it's verifiable. All of it's devastating.

If you're a dog owner reading that review, you're not taking the risk. You're moving on to the next product. That's exactly what the attacker wants.

The black hat operators have gotten smarter because guys like me have spent years on podcasts explaining what we look for. 

They listened. They adapted. They tested what gets caught and what doesn't... and now they've got a formula.

So if you're still approaching review attacks the same way you did two years ago, you're bringing a checklist to a chess match.

I sat down with Chris McCabe on Seller Performance Solutions to break down exactly what's changed, what the new attack patterns look like, and how sellers need to think about defense in 2026.

Posted Ago: 2 days ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
You need to think of your listing as a storefront, NOT a product page.

And the most common mistake sellers make is writing it for an algorithm instead of a human being.

Keyword-stuffed titles like "Spatula Silicone Non-Stick Heat Resistant BPA Free Blue Red Green Large Small" might check indexing boxes. But they scream low quality to the shopper scrolling past.

Strong listing copy does three things: 

#1 - States what the product is

#2 - Explains why it's better

#3 - Removes purchase anxiety. 

That's it. When you do those three things well, SEO actually improves naturally (because conversion improves).

Your average Amazon shopper doesn't care how many keywords you hit. They care whether they trust you. And keyword stuffing makes your brand feel cheap, even if your product isn't.

Same goes for images. Shoppers can't touch your product. So your visuals carry the entire burden of perceived quality. If your main image looks like it was shot on a kitchen counter with an iPhone 6, you're losing the sale before anyone reads a single bullet point.

Treat your listings like assets that need protection and refinement.

Amazon sellers treat their image stack like a photo album.

Your 7 image slots should work like a sales funnel. Each slide has a job. Each one moves the customer closer to buying.

Slide one gets the click. 

Slide two shows the core benefit (not the spec, the benefit). "3 full days of power" hits different than "10,000mAh battery." 

Slide three builds trust. 

Slide four handles the objection. 

And so on.

One message per slide. Not five points crammed into one graphic that nobody can read on their phone.

If your images aren't doing a job, they're taking up space.

Author Title: Founder @TraceFuse.ai | The Amazon Review Expert | E-commerce Strategist | Influencer Marketing Specialist | Keynote Speaker
Posted Ago: 3 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
One of the top reasons Amazon sellers get negative reviews:

Product size... not quality.

Customer opens the box, the product is smaller (or bigger) than they expected...

And now you've got a one-star review that says "way smaller than I thought" sitting on your listing forever.

The fix is dead simple. 

Stop relying on dimensions alone. 

Nobody reads "7.5 x 4.2 x 3.1 inches" and actually pictures the product in their hands.

Put it next to something people recognize. 

A phone. A coffee mug. A human hand. Instant scale. 

Think of the classic "banana for scale" meme.

One image slot dedicated to comparative sizing can save you from dozens of "not as described" returns and the reviews that come with them.

Author Title: Founder @TraceFuse.ai | The Amazon Review Expert | E-commerce Strategist | Influencer Marketing Specialist | Keynote Speaker
Posted Ago: 14 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
Your price tells a story whether you want it to or not.

When prices swing too often, shoppers start asking themselves uncomfortable questions: 

"Is this overpriced?" 

"Is this a cheap product pretending to be premium?" 

"Should I just wait for a discount?"

Even if your product is excellent, unstable pricing makes your brand feel unstable.

If you're positioned as premium, constant discounting erodes that position. If you're value-driven, erratic price spikes kill conversion. Either way, inconsistency is the enemy.

And it gets worse when your distribution leaks. Wholesale partners undercutting your online pricing. DTC discounts making your Amazon storefront look overpriced. Unauthorized resellers creating outright price chaos.

None of this is a "pricing problem." It's a brand management problem. Because every time a shopper sees your product at a wildly different price point than last week, a small piece of trust evaporates. And trust, once lost on Amazon, is brutally hard to rebuild.

Pricing strategy isn't just about margin. It's about what your price communicates about who you are.

Content:
"Just use AI for that."

I hear this constantly. And it's almost always wrong.

Specifically when it comes to Amazon review removal.

I've talked to hundreds of sellers who tried the AI route. 

Fed prompts into ChatGPT. 

Generated case filings. 

Hit submit and waited...

Legend has it, they're still waiting til this day, lol.

And I'm not anti-AI. We've been building our own AI tools at TraceFuse for three years (before most people even knew what a prompt was). We use AI every day.

But AI is only as good as the information you put into it.

If you don't understand how Amazon's case system works, what format their team responds to, and what's changed in the last 90 days... your prompt is garbage. And AI will polish that garbage into a very professional-sounding document that still gets ignored.

The target moves constantly. 
Amazon changes how they evaluate cases. 
New policies roll out. 
Internal teams shift priorities. 
What worked three months ago might get flagged today.

AI isn't in the trenches seeing those changes happen in real time. People are.

People who are filing cases every single day, reading every response, tracking which arguments land and which ones don't. That institutional knowledge doesn't live in a language model. It lives in experience.

And there's the accountability piece nobody talks about. If AI gets it wrong and your listing tanks, who's responsible? 

AI doesn't give refunds. AI doesn't have a reputation on the line. AI doesn't pick up the phone.

AI is a tool. A powerful one. But a tool without expertise behind it is just an expensive way to get the same bad result faster.

Where have you seen AI overpromise and underdeliver in your business?

Posted Ago: 4 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
Too many Amazon sellers make themselves a "soft target."

If someone broke into your safe and stole $1,000 (and you didn't change the lock) how fast do you think they'd be back?

I couldn't get back to your house fast enough. Word would get out, making you every cat burglar's dream.

That's exactly what's happening with review attacks on Amazon.

A competitor hits your listing with fake negatives. 

Your star rating drops. 
Your conversion tanks. 
They gain market share. 

And you go, "Eh, it's probably a one-time thing." You moved on.

But if it worked, they're coming back.

This is soft target vs. hard target, and most sellers are walking around with the vault wide open.

The people running these attacks don't just randomly pick a listing. 

They've studied the ecosystem. 
They've tested what gets through Amazon's filters. 
They've done trial and error until they found what sticks. 

This is methodical.

And when they see a brand that doesn't fight back? That brand goes on the rotation.

It's the same logic as a bully. Ignoring the problem doesn't make it go away. You can bury your head in the sand (and that's fine for a minute) but when you pull it back out, the same problem is standing right there waiting for you. Plus a few new ones.

When you draw the line, remove the reviews that are already there, you signal that your brand isn't an easy mark.

Get the fraudulent reviews removed. Monitor for new ones. Have a system in place so that when the next attack comes—and it will—you're not scrambling.

Be the hard target.

TraceFuse can help with that.
Author Title: Founder @TraceFuse.ai | The Amazon Review Expert | E-commerce Strategist | Influencer Marketing Specialist | Keynote Speaker
Posted Ago: 3 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
A single "broke after a week" review can stop conversion cold... even if 200 other customers are happy.

That's the asymmetry of reviews on Amazon. 

Shoppers seek reasons to avoid regret (maybe even more than they seek reasons to buy). And negative reviews carry disproportionate psychological weight.

Which means your reviews are the most fragile brand asset you have. They're public, permanent, and psychologically powerful.

And the more visible your brand becomes, the more likely you are to be targeted (by fake reviews, review bombing, and competitors testing the boundaries of Amazon's policies).

Brand management means building systems around this reality. 

Early detection when a 1-star drops. 

Proactive resolution through proper channels. If you want this part done right, hit us up here at TraceFuse.

And treating reviews as a product R&D feedback loop (if customers keep complaining about the same thing, fix it in the next production run).

Sellers who treat reviews as "just something that happens to them" put themselves at risk. The "win some, lose some" approach will always lose in the aggregate.

But if you take action to monitor and protect your listing, you will be miles ahead of your competitors.

The "wild west" is being tamed.

Amazon sued the operators of 75+ websites last year for selling fake reviews. And they're not slowing down.

They went after Facebook group admins. 
Telegram brokers. 
Services that sold fake "Helpful" votes. 
Services that mass-reported competitors' legitimate reviews to get them removed. 

Even a company that sold entire pre-built Amazon seller accounts to previously suspended sellers.

And the lawsuits didn't just result in slaps on the wrist. Amazon got court orders to seize website domains. They won monetary judgments exceeding the annual revenue of some of these sellers.

Let me say that differently... some sellers lost more money in the lawsuit than they ever made selling on Amazon.

I get why people are tempted. You're sitting at 3.9 stars. Your competitor is at 4.5. You know some of their reviews look suspicious. And some guy in a Facebook group says he can get you 50 verified five-stars for $500. Quick fix.

That "quick fix" now comes with the possibility of Amazon's legal team knocking on your door. Not just a suspension. A lawsuit. With damages.

And the detection is getting better every quarter. Amazon's fraud AI flags review patterns that humans would never catch (timing anomalies, reviewer account clusters, purchase-to-review velocity that doesn't match organic behavior). At TraceFuse, we've been deploying a similar technology for years to flag TOS violations automatically.

The sellers who are building durable brands on Amazon in 2026 are CLEANING, rather than gaming, the system. Identifying reviews that violate TOS. Getting illegitimate negatives removed through compliant channels. Building organic review velocity through better post-purchase experience.

It's slower. It's less sexy than a Telegram group promising overnight results. But it's the only approach that doesn't come with an expiration date... or a subpoena.

Starting this week, Amazon is rolling out a massive update to how reviews are shared across product variations. If your parent ASIN has child variations that differ in size, formula, model, or function, those reviews no longer get pooled together.

That 4.6 star rating you've been sitting on? It might be a 3.8 by May.

For years, sellers built their review strategy around variation stacking. Launch a new flavor, merge it into the parent, and ride the existing reviews to instant credibility. Amazon just closed that door.

And they're not being subtle about it. They're rolling this out category by category between now and May 31st. You'll get 30 days notice before it hits your listings. But by the time that email lands in your inbox, you should already have a plan.

I've been telling sellers for years: your review profile isn't just a vanity metric. It's infrastructure. It's what holds your conversion rate, your ad efficiency, and your organic ranking together. When one piece shifts, everything downstream moves with it.

So what do you do right now?

Audit your parent-child relationships. If you merged ASINs that deliver genuinely different customer experiences, those reviews are getting separated whether you like it or not. Better to restructure on your terms than wait for Amazon to do it for you.

Look at which child ASINs are carrying your rating and which ones are dragging it down. If you've got a variation with 200 five-star reviews propping up a variation with a 3.2 average... that math is about to change.

And start building review velocity on your weaker ASINs now. Not through manipulation. Through better follow-up sequences, better packaging inserts, better post-purchase experience. The brands that already have strong review fundamentals across every variation won't feel this at all.

Content:
Your product could be flawless and your business could still be bleeding.

I always use this example: Imagine you own a Michelin-star restaurant. Incredible food. Perfect location. Five-star ambiance.

But your hostess is rude to 30 people a week... and you have no idea.

Then the reviews start trickling in. 

"Jennifer was awful." 

"The hostess yelled at us." 

"Never going back because of the front desk."

You're reading these going, "What is happening? Our food is incredible." 

Meanwhile, you've got a Jennifer problem that's torching your reputation in real time.

This happens with Amazon sellers more than you'd think. The product is great, but the packaging is flimsy. Or the instructions are confusing. Or the sizing chart is off. Small things. Fixable things. But if you're ignoring your negative reviews, you'll never see it.

I talk to sellers all the time who treat bad reviews like background noise. 

"It comes with the territory." 

Sure. But buried inside those 1 and 2-star reviews might be intel that could transform your listing.

Not every negative review is an attack. Some of them are free consulting.

Read them. Sort them. Look for patterns. If three people mention the same issue in 30 days, that's a signal.

The sellers who win long-term aren't the ones with zero bad reviews. They're the ones who actually listen to them.

Content:
Amazon suspended your account and you wrote an appeal that starts with: 

"This suspension is unfair and we have done nothing wrong."

You just guaranteed yourself a rejection.

I see this constantly. Sellers who are genuinely good operators, selling legitimate products, running clean businesses... 

And they blow their appeal because they write it like a defense attorney instead of an operations manager.

Amazon's appeal reviewers read thousands of these a week. They're not looking for passion. They're not looking for a convincing argument. They're looking for three things: what broke, what you fixed, and what system you built so it doesn't break again.

The moment you shift into blame mode (blaming the customer, blaming a competitor, blaming Amazon's own system) you've told the reviewer that you don't actually understand the problem. And if you don't understand the problem, why would they trust you to fix it?

The appeals that work are almost boring to read. 

They're specific. 
They're factual. 
They reference exact ASINs, exact dates, exact process changes. 

No emotion. 
No excuses. 

Boil it down to "this happened, we did this to correct it, and now this system is in place."

Precision beats persuasion every single time.

Has anyone here been through a suspension appeal? What was the hardest part of the process? 👇

Posted Ago: 3 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
Amazon can blacklist you for asking too many questions.

Not about reviews. Not about products. About your own support cases.

I see sellers do this all the time. They file a case to get a review removed. It's a legitimate TOS violation (profanity, competitor mention, shipping complaint on an FBA product). Open and shut.

Then 48 hours later: "Hey Amazon, any update?"

Then 72 hours later: "Just checking in on case #4829371."

Then again. And again.

And suddenly their case goes cold. No response. No removal. Nothing.

They lost because they were impatient.

Amazon has internal timelines. When you keep pinging them for updates, you don't move to the front of the line... you get flagged as a nuisance. I've seen sellers get their case escalation privileges effectively frozen because they couldn't stop hitting the follow-up button.

It took my team two years to figure out the timing on this stuff. 

When to file. 
Which department to file with. 
What terminology to use. 
And critically: how long to wait.

For brand registry cases, we wait at least 10 days before any follow-up. Sometimes a review gets removed in 72 hours. Sometimes it takes two months. And sometimes it doesn't get removed at all.

That's the reality. But the sellers who play the long game and follow the process get dramatically better results than the ones who spam Amazon's inbox.

Patience is literally a competitive advantage on this platform.

Content:
You're selling 300 units a day on Amazon and only clicking the "request a review" button 3 times.

That math doesn't work.

In the back end of Seller Central, every single order has a button you can click to send a review request to the buyer. Amazon doesn't do it automatically. YOU have to trigger it.

And when you're moving serious volume, doing that manually is basically impossible. So most sellers just… don't.

They sell 500 units in a week. Maybe 10 people get a review request. Maybe 2 actually leave one. And then they wonder why their review count isn't growing.

It's not a product problem. It's a distribution problem.

You're not getting bad reviews because your product is bad. You're getting too few reviews because you're not asking enough people.

And here's the uncomfortable truth about buyer behavior:

Someone who had a bad experience is way more likely to leave a review unprompted than someone who had a great one. 

The angry buyer goes straight to your listing. 

The happy buyer goes on with their day, makes some sandwiches, deals with the kids, and forgets you exist.

So if you're not actively requesting reviews from every buyer, you're letting the angry minority define your listing.

There are fully Amazon-compliant tools that automate this for $20-40 a month. 

They click that button for you on every order after a set number of days. Without them, you are leaving opportunity on the floor.

Posted Ago: 2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
Unless you are selling rap albums, online drama does not sell tickets.

Still, see business owners doing this constantly.

They get a negative review. Their ego takes a hit. They decide to "clap back" in the comments.

They want to be right. But being right on the internet usually means losing money.

Here is the rule I tell my team (and my son):

The Mother Test.

Before you hit reply to that angry customer:

Content:
A seller shipped out a brand new product. Got back a box of rocks.

Not figuratively. Literally a different item stuffed in the original packaging and sent back as a "return."

Amazon refunded the buyer immediately. The seller ate the loss.

This is called product switching, and it's one of the most common forms of return fraud on Amazon. A buyer orders your product, swaps it with something cheaper or broken, sends it back, and walks away with a full refund and your inventory.

For FBA sellers, it's even worse. You don't control the inspection process. Amazon receives the return, maybe glances at it, marks it as "customer damaged" or restockable, and moves on. By the time you notice your inventory count doesn't add up, the window to dispute it is already closing.

And it's not just the refund that hurts. You're absorbing outbound shipping, return shipping, referral fees, and fulfillment fees (most of which Amazon doesn't reimburse automatically). On a low-margin product, one fraudulent return can wipe out the profit from 5 or 10 clean sales.

The sellers who handle this well aren't the ones who panic over every case. They're the ones tracking return patterns by ASIN, filing reimbursement claims in weekly batches, and watching for cost spikes that don't match sales growth.

Fraud doesn't announce itself. It shows up as quiet margin erosion over weeks and months.

I wrote a full breakdown on how to spot it, prevent it, and handle it within Amazon's rules

Some customers are renting your products for free. They just don't call it that.

They buy a ring light for a photoshoot on Saturday. Return it Monday. Full refund.

They order a dress for a wedding. Wear it once. Send it back with the tags still on.

They grab a portable speaker for a camping trip. Bring it home Sunday night. "Not as described."

Or in the case of this review... used, abused, and returned a plunger??? Gross.

This is called wardrobing. And on Amazon, it's almost impossible to fight on a case-by-case basis because each individual return looks legitimate.

The damage shows up over time. Your return rate creeps up. Units come back in worse condition. Your margins quietly shrink and you can't pinpoint exactly where the bleeding started.

The sellers who get ahead of this don't try to catch every bad actor. They look at patterns. Which ASINs have abnormally high return rates compared to similar products? Which return reasons keep repeating without matching what reviews actually say? Where are cost spikes showing up without a corresponding jump in sales?

That's signal. And once you see it, you can make smart decisions. Adjust packaging to include tamper-evident seals. Bundle products so single-item abuse gets harder. Shift high-risk SKUs from FBA to FBM where you control the inspection.

You can't stop every fraudulent return. But you can make your business a much harder target.

What's the wildest return you've ever received on Amazon? I know some of you have stories. 👇

Author Title: Founder @TraceFuse.ai | The Amazon Review Expert | E-commerce Strategist | Influencer Marketing Specialist | Keynote Speaker

Content:
Amazon does not care that a 1-star review hurt your feelings. 

They don't care that the customer is lying. 

They don't care that it's unfair.

They only care if the review violates their TOS.

Stop filing cases based on emotion. Amazon is a machine, and you have to speak machine language.

We spent two years cracking the code on how to talk to Amazon's internal teams.

It’s not just about clicking "Report Abuse." It’s about the phrasing.

If a customer writes: 

"This product is garbage and the seller is a thief," Amazon's bot sees that as a valid customer opinion.

But if you frame the case correctly (highlighting that the review contains "unverifiable claims" or violates "community standards on respectful communication") suddenly you get a different result.

You have to guide the support agent (or the bot) to the exact clause in their own Terms of Service that the review violates.

Don't ask Amazon for a favor. 

Show Amazon where critical reviews are breaking their own rules.

Because Amazon sets storage limits for Q2 based on your performance now.

If you ignore this, you’re going to wake up in March trying to launch a new product, and Amazon is going to tell you: 

"Sorry, your limit is 1,000 units."

You will be handcuffed by your own inefficiency.

Monitor your IPI score weekly. 

If it's dipping, run a promo. 

Move the units. 

Protect your future capacity.

Amazon wants thorough reviews. They want context.

If I tell you "the weather is bad," that doesn't help you. 

If I tell you "it's 40 degrees and raining," now you know to grab a coat.

We look for reviews that lack that "40 degrees and raining" detail.

Does it mention the product features? 
Does it explain the failure? 
Or is it just a one-line "hate post"?

Amazon’s LLMs are getting smarter at identifying these low-value comments.

We just help point them in the right direction.

Stop letting low-context "noise" drag down your star rating.

Your product deserves better than a one-word insult.

Content:
Amazon SEO is quietly changing. Most listings aren’t ready.

I tested Amazon’s AI shopping assistant, Rufus, with a question, not a keyword.

“What boots are best for wide feet on rocky terrain?”

I didn’t search “hiking boots.”
I didn’t filter by best-seller.

Rufus returned a short list of products that clearly addressed wide feet and rocky terrain in their content.

What stood out:

The winning listings weren’t necessarily keyword-stuffed. 

They had reviews and Q&A that explicitly mentioned the use case.

That’s the shift.

Amazon’s AI tools synthesize context from:

Product detail pages
Customer reviews
Customer Questions & Answers

When a shopper asks a nuanced question, Rufus looks for listings that already answer it in natural language.

What this means for brands in 2026...

If you’re still writing listings for a search bar, you’re behind.
You need to write for an answer engine.
One of the most underused assets on a listing is the Customer Questions section.

Instead of waiting for random questions:

Proactively identify common use cases and objections
Make sure your listing clearly addresses them
Answer real customer questions thoroughly and accurately

Examples:

“Is this safe to put in the dishwasher?”
“Does this work for people with sensitive skin?”
“Is this comfortable for wide feet on uneven terrain?”

These questions do two things:

They help real shoppers convert
They give Amazon’s AI clearer context about when and for whom your product is a good fit

Listings with empty or vague Q&A aren’t invisible... but they’re at a disadvantage when shoppers ask more specific, conversational questions.

Context beats keyword density.
Clarity beats cleverness.

And the best way to rank for complex queries is to already be answering them.

Because if you can't describe your product like a normal human being...

Normal human beings will never want to buy it.

Author Title: Founder @TraceFuse.ai | The Amazon Review Expert | E-commerce Strategist | Influencer Marketing Specialist | Keynote Speaker
Posted Ago: 1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
Sometimes it is cheaper to let the customer keep the product.

I know. It hurts your soul.

You paid for the inventory. It feels like you are being scammed.

But let's look at the actual math on a $25 item...

If a customer returns it:

You refund the $25.
You pay Amazon $3-$5 for the return shipping label.
You pay a processing fee.
The unit likely comes back "Customer Damaged" and goes to unfulfillable inventory.

Total loss: $30+ and you lost the stock.

Now, look at the "Returnless Resolution" setting in Seller Central.

If you set a rule to offer a full refund without return for items under a certain dollar amount (or specific heavy/bulky SKUs):

You refund the $25.
The customer is happy (likely removes any critical review... may even leave a 5-star review for "great service").
You pay zero logistics fees.

Total loss: $25.

You just saved $5+ per return by swallowing your pride.

Check your "Return Settings" today. 

If your return logistics cost more than your COGS, you are doing it wrong.

You stop trying to be a salesperson and start acting like a compliance officer.

Posted Ago: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
It's a tough pill to swallow, but sellers need to remember: 

Amazon doesn't work for you.

People file a case with Amazon expecting instant responses. 

Updates every 6 hours. 

Reviews removed by morning.

Then they get frustrated when Amazon goes silent for 10 days.

Here's what they don't understand: Amazon has millions of sellers. You're not special to them.

And bombarding them with update requests? That hurts your case.

We learned this over two years of testing.

The process that works:

→ File your case 
→ Give Amazon the appropriate time 
→ Wait the full 10 days before asking for updates 
→ Stay factual, not emotional

Most sellers fail at step three. They can't handle the silence.

Day 2: "Any updates?"
Day 4: "Can you check on this?"
Day 6: "This is urgent!"

Amazon sees that and deprioritizes you.

When we file with Brand Registry, we wait exactly 10 days. 

That's their internal response window. Respect it.

The other mistake? Getting emotional.

"This review is destroying my business! My family depends on this! You need to fix this NOW!"

Amazon doesn't care about your emotions. They care about facts.

Which guideline was violated? Where's the evidence? What's the specific policy?

Bring receipts, not feelings.

Patience and compliance beat urgency and emotion every single time.

Have you been approaching Amazon with facts or feelings?

Posted Ago: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
Here's one place AI has made life harder:

Five years ago, fake reviews were sloppy.

Broken English. Posted in batches. Same phrasing across multiple accounts.

Amazon caught most of them before they went live.

Today? Attacks are sophisticated.

They space out timing. Use real purchase histories. Write reviews that sound completely legitimate.

The only violation might be one line mentioning your competitor's brand name.

That's it. Everything else reads authentic.

This is why manual review matters.

Amazon's system looks for patterns at scale. 

When attacks are engineered to avoid those patterns, they slip through.

We've seen:

→ Verified purchases that are clearly brushing but disguised across months
→ Reviews that bury violations in paragraph three after two paragraphs of "helpful" content
→ Coordinated attacks using 10+ different buyer accounts with zero connection on the surface

The sophistication is wild.

Competitors aren't just hiring cheap click farms anymore. 

They're using services that understand Amazon's detection systems.

So reviews get through that technically violate guidelines, but they're written carefully enough that most sellers miss the violation entirely.

They see a one-star and think, "Well, that's just someone who didn't like my product."

Meanwhile, buried in that review is a mention of pricing (violation) or a comparison to a competitor (violation) or a complaint about FBA shipping (violation).

The evolution forced us to get better too.

We can't just scan for obvious red flags. 

We read every review thoroughly because that's where the violations hide now.

In plain sight. Disguised as legitimate criticism.

Anyone still using 2019 tactics to monitor reviews is getting destroyed.

The attacks leveled up. Your defense needs to match.

They're not just posting fake critical reviews anymore. 

They're gaming the social proof that makes Amazon think the review matters.

Here's what actually happens:

Competitor posts a violation-heavy review (mentions your price, cusses, talks about FBA shipping issues).

Then they pay a click farm to mark it helpful 50-100 times.

Amazon's system flags it: "High engagement. Users find this valuable."

Now when we file to remove it, there's friction. 

Amazon pushes back because the data says people care about this review.

Even though the review clearly violates guidelines.

Even though the "helpful" clicks are just as fake as the review itself.

The workaround?

Get to reviews before anyone can mark them helpful.

This is why monitoring daily matters. 

A fresh violation with zero engagement? 

We can file immediately and get it down fast.

That same review after 100 "helpful" clicks? 

Still removable, but it takes longer because we're fighting manufactured social proof.

If you're doing weekly review checks, you're already behind.

The attacks happen fast. 

The manipulation happens faster.

By the time you notice, the damage is baked into Amazon's perception of that review's value.

Daily monitoring is the only way to catch this before the helpful buttons get weaponized.

How often are you checking your reviews? Weekly? Daily? Never?

Content:
"How long does it take to remove reviews?"

My best honest answer? 24 hours to 24 weeks.

I know. Terrible answer. But it's reality.

The timeline depends on:

- What the review actually says
- Which Amazon department we're filing with
- Whether the rep we get actually reads the case or just clicks "deny"

We've had clients sign up and see reviews removed in 48 hours.

We've had others take months.

If someone leaves a review that says "this effing product broke after one use" that's a clear violation. We'll keep filing until it comes down.

But we have a third party involved, Amazon. And they're a big company with a lot on their plate. We work on their timeline.

We can't wave a wand and make it happen instantly.

What we can do:

→ File cases manually (no AI shortcuts)
→ Know exactly which departments to escalate to
→ Use the terminology that actually gets Amazon's attention
→ Keep filing until we get the right rep who agrees with us

That's why we're success-based. You only pay for reviews that actually get removed.

If it takes 24 weeks, I want it down just as badly as you do. Once it's down, we have happy customers and get paid.

But I'm not going to lie and promise 5-day turnarounds when there's a whole process involved.

We've removed over 16,000 reviews across 700 brands.

Some fast. Some slow. 

All eventually gone because they violated Amazon's guidelines.

If someone's promising you instant results, they're either lying or they're about to get your account suspended.

We play the long game. 

We play it right. 

And we win consistently.

Just not always on day one.

You know what a RICO case is?

Federal prosecutors used it to take down entire mob operations by connecting the dots between seemingly unrelated crimes.

We do the same thing with fake reviews.

Let's say one buyer account makes five purchases of your product... and leaves five one-star reviews.

Who buys a product they hate five times?

Nobody.

But when you only have 10 critical reviews total, that pattern gets buried. It looks random.

When you have 100+ critical reviews, we can spot it immediately.

Same buyer account attacking multiple ASINs.

Same buyer only leaving five-stars for your competitor's identical products.

Purchase patterns that make zero sense for a real customer.

We flag it. 

Document it. 

Build the case. 

File with Amazon.

More data = more connections we can make.

That's why sellers with larger review counts actually have an advantage when it comes to removal. We can tie the fraud together and show Amazon the pattern.

The same buyer hit you six times in two months? That's not bad luck. That's fraud.

And fraud violates Amazon's guidelines.

We just need enough data points to prove it.

If you're getting hit repeatedly and you can feel something's off?

Let us look at the buyer patterns. 

We'll show you exactly what's happening.

Then we'll build the case that gets them removed.

Content:
A competitor just spent $300 to destroy your $30 eyeliner listing.

Makes zero sense until you do the math.

That customer uses one tube per month.

20 years of loyalty = $7,200 lifetime value.

So yeah, they'll spend $300 on brushing attacks to steal that customer.

This is why beauty, supplements, and pet products get attacked more than high-ticket items. 

The lifetime value math makes fraud profitable.

Here's how it works:

→ Competitor buys your product through a verified account
→ Ships it to a random address (this is called "brushing")
→ Now they're a verified purchaser
→ Leaves a fake critical review
→ Your rating drops, their BSR climbs

A $5,000 bike? Nobody's running that play. You buy one every 5-10 years. The unit economics don't make sense.

But consumables with high repeat purchase rates?

That's where the attacks happen.

We've removed over 16,000 reviews across 700 brands. Most of these clients sell high lifetime value products that get targeted relentlessly.

If you're in beauty, supplements, or pet supplies and you're not monitoring your reviews weekly, you're leaving the door wide open.

The good news? These attacks violate Amazon's guidelines. You just need to know how to file the cases correctly.

Most sellers wait until Prime Day or Q4 (right around this time of year) to panic.

The smart ones clean up their listings proactively and monitor daily moving forward.

But no matter what, it's better to start thinking about this TODAY.

And if they didn't? They probably went out of business.

Every piece of feedback, even if it's negative, is valuable. If you know what to listen for, it will always lead you to a stronger, more successful product.

A one-star review feels like getting punched in the face. I get it.

But if three people say the same thing in one week?

That's not an attack. That's product development research you didn't have to pay for.

Maybe your packaging sucks.

Maybe people don't know how to use the product.

Maybe there's an actual defect you haven't caught yet.

You can ignore it and keep getting returns.

Or you can read between the lines and fix what's actually wrong.

Now, if someone's leaving reviews that say "this is an effing terrible product" or mentioning your competitors by name, that's different.

Those violate Amazon's guidelines and should be removed.

But genuine feedback? Even when it stings?

That's literally your customers telling you how to make more money.

The sellers who've been around for a decade didn't get lucky.

They listened, iterated, and improved based on what real users were saying.

Take the ego hit. 

Extract the lesson. 

Make the product better.

Then watch your rating climb naturally because you actually solved the problem.

That's how you build a brand instead of just flipping products.

Don't put it in a lake. 

Don't sell it to unsuspecting customers.

Just take the loss and move on.

I couldn't understand why they'd risk someone's health for a few thousand units.

Author Title: Founder @TraceFuse.ai | The Amazon Review Expert | E-commerce Strategist | Influencer Marketing Specialist | Keynote Speaker
Posted Ago: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
🧪 Wired controller fails to be… wireless 😅

A 1-star reads: “Does not connect wirelessly to my Xbox.”

If your Xbox listing clearly indicates it is WIRED — in the title, bullets, or hero image — this is a non-product expectation issue. File it for removal with a clean screenshot, permalink, and timestamp.

But if your listing doesn’t clearly say so, that’s on the seller.

The quick fix:

- Title: Spec first, features later (“Xbox Controller — Wired, USB-A/USB-C”)
- Hero image: Show the cable plugged in
- On-image badge: “Requires cable (included) — not Bluetooth”

The goal isn’t to call out buyers — it’s to make the listing accurate so shoppers know exactly what to expect.

At TraceFuse, we help brands remove negative reviews ethically with a ToS-compliant process. We've done it for over 700 brands, removing 16,000+ invalid reviews.

Ever had a funny “failed expectation” review in your listings lately? (No brand names!)

At TraceFuse, this is one way we help brands stabilize ratings — by classifying and reporting reviews that fall outside Amazon’s product-focused guidelines.

1️⃣ Identify what the review actually evaluates.
2️⃣ Gather proof — quote, screenshot, timestamp.
3️⃣ File a report, track outcomes, and stay consistent.

Remove only what breaks the rules.

Content:
Did your top-selling Amazon product just get nuked by a sketchy wave of reviews? 

Here’s the recovery plan I give clients.

First, breathe.

We’ve helped 700+ brands through this—16,000+ removals and counting.

Step 1 — Measure the hit

What was your rating before vs. after?

What’s the conversion drop (yesterday vs. last week)?

Which SKUs/variations took the biggest dive?


Step 2 — Triage the targets

File only what violates policy first:

Pricing opinions (“$24.99 is too expensive”)

Competitor mentions (“Brand X is cheaper/better”)

FBA delivery/warehouse gripes

Profanity/personal attacks 

Keep truthful product defects (they’re R&D).


Step 3 — File cleanly (and in waves)

Quote the exact sentence

Attach screenshot + review link + timestamp

5–10 cases at a time → track approvals → refile once if miscategorized


Step 4 — Set a real timeline

Some removals: days.

Others: weeks.

Stubborn cases: months.

Plan cash flow and inventory with this in mind.


Step 5 — Monitor while you recover

Daily → hourly during peak weeks

Flag new hits fast; don’t let bad data harden on the page

Document patterns for future filings (and product fixes)


This hurts now, but it’s fixable.

Remove what never belonged. Improve what’s true.

That’s how ratings stabilize—and rank and conversion come back.

Content:
"Your bounce rate is 70%! That's why your rankings are dropping!"

Complete nonsense.

Bounce rate measures user behavior, not content quality. 

Someone might find exactly what they need on your page and leave immediately.

Perfect user experience. High bounce rate.

Google knows this. That's why they focus on different signals entirely.

Time to valuable action. 

Task completion rate. 

Return visit patterns.

A user who bounces after getting their question answered is more valuable than a user who clicks through five pages but never finds what they need.

The bounce rate obsession creates backwards optimization.

Businesses add unnecessary navigation, force multi-page experiences, and create artificial engagement just to reduce bounce rates.

Meanwhile, they're making their sites harder to use for people who know exactly what they want.

The best landing pages often have high bounce rates because they convert visitors immediately.

Content:
I went to five different colleges before graduating in 2003.

Most people would say I was indecisive or unfocused. 

But I'm better off because I took my own path through education.

Each school taught me something different about learning, people, and environments. 

Some were too rigid. 

Others too chaotic. 

Some focused on theory while I craved practical application.

I was also running businesses while in school, which meant I needed flexibility that traditional academic programs couldn't provide.

Sometimes what looks like inconsistency is actually optimization.

I wasn't failing at college. 

I was testing different approaches to education until I found what worked for my learning style and life circumstances.

This experience shaped how I think about business decisions now.

When people see that we switched from retainer pricing to performance-driven pricing, they might think we were inconsistent. 

But we were actually iterating toward the optimal model.

When we moved from manual review analysis to AI-assisted detection, it wasn't because our original approach was wrong. 

It was because we found a better way.

The entrepreneurial journey rarely follows a straight line. 

What looks like pivoting or changing directions is often just continuous optimization.

Traditional education teaches you to pick a major and stick with it. 

Entrepreneurship teaches you to keep experimenting until you find what works.

The key is knowing the difference between productive iteration and aimless wandering.

Productive iteration has a clear goal and measures progress toward that goal. 

Aimless wandering just keeps moving without learning.

My college experience was productive iteration. 

I was optimizing for the right learning environment while building real businesses.

Some paths look messy from the outside but make perfect sense when you understand the underlying strategy.

"This seller doesn't respect our process."

So we move on, find the channel that's working right now, and always respect the people on the other end (Amazon has a lot of cases to file, and their team works very hard).

Amazon doesn't work for you. You work within their system.

Act accordingly.

Content:
Your customer ordered a supplement. 

Amazon's warehouse shipped them a broken bottle.

They leave you a 1-star review: "Arrived damaged, powder everywhere."

This isn't a product review. This is a fulfillment problem.

Yet it's destroying your listing's conversion rate.

Most sellers just accept these reviews as part of doing business on Amazon.

But that couldn't be further from the truth!

FBA-related complaints are actually removable because they violate Amazon's review guidelines:

- Late shipping issues
- Damaged packaging
- Wrong items sent
- Used products in new condition

None of these reflect your actual product quality.

These reviews should be hitting Amazon's logistics team, not your product listing.

We've successfully removed thousands of FBA-related reviews because Amazon recognizes they're warehouse issues, not seller issues.

The key is knowing how to position the removal request properly.

You can't just say "this is FBA's fault." 

You need to reference the specific guideline violations and use Amazon's internal terminology.

Your product quality shouldn't suffer because of warehouse mistakes.

Type 1: Legitimate criticism

Take the feedback. Improve your product. 

Version 2.0, 3.0, 4.0... every successful product evolves based on customer input.

Type 2: Policy violations 

These need to go. 

Reviews with profanity, pricing complaints, competitor mentions, FBA shipping issues.

Most sellers make the mistake of trying to fight every negative review.

Don't do that.

Focus your energy on the reviews that actually violate Amazon's guidelines.

We only go after reviews we can win. 

That's why TraceFuse is performance-based. You only pay when reviews actually get removed.

In 3 years, we've removed over 14,000 reviews across 600+ brands.

We know the difference between constructive criticism and policy violations.

Accept the feedback.

Content:
We call them "sock puppet accounts."

Fake buyer accounts controlled by competitors who buy your products just to destroy you with one-star reviews.

Here's how we spot them:

They buy your flip-flops and give you one star. 

Then they buy your competitor's nearly identical sandals and give five stars. 

And within these reviews you start to see the same, copy-and-pasted phrases: 

"This is the worst product in the world!!!!" (always with four exclamation marks). 

A sock puppet account may buy your product five times (just to leave five one-star reviews). 

This is one of the most tell-tale signs of foul play... normal customers don't repeatedly buy products they hate.

The most obvious tell? Review velocity. 

Let's say you normally get one review per day, then suddenly get ten negative reviews daily for five straight days. 

Your sales didn't increase 10x, so where did these buyers come from?

These aren't random angry customers... come out of the woodworks on a random Tuesday. 

These are calculated business attacks designed to knock you out of the buy box and steal your market share.

Google it. This stuff happens more than you think.

Are you monitoring for sock puppet attacks?

Reviews mentioning competitors. 

Reviews talking about pricing. 

Anything FBA-related (if something shipped late, broken, or used, that's Amazon's warehouse issue, not the seller's fault).

We go after the most obvious violations first. People using the F-word in reviews. If I showed that to 100 Amazonians, they'd all say "yeah, that shouldn't be there."

Those are the ones with no debate. Clear violations that Amazon will remove when properly reported.

We've cracked this code over three years of machine learning...

With an AI we built specifically for Amazon's review system.

That's how we've removed over 14,000 reviews for 600+ brands.

The crazy part? Most sellers don't even know these rules exist. They just accept bad reviews as "part of doing business."

Look, if someone leaves a legitimate review but uses profanity or mentions pricing, we're still going to file on it. Those are Amazon's rules, not ours.

We're just leveling the playing field and helping sellers use every tool Amazon gives them.

Why wouldn't you?

The technical ignorance gets worse when they can't diagnose why new content isn't getting indexed, never realizing they blocked the crawlers from the folders where that content lives.

Content:
Is your SEO strategy falling behind?

Your competitors are getting featured snippets, star ratings, and enhanced search listings...

While your perfectly optimized content gets basic blue links.

The difference? Structured data implementation that most agencies either ignore or botch completely.

Schema markup is what tells Google exactly what your content means, not just what it says. 

It's the difference between Google guessing that your page is about local businesses versus Google knowing you're a specific type of business with exact hours, services, and location data.

Most agencies skip structured data because it requires technical knowledge they don't have.

For these, Tracefuse uses a thorough review removal process that helps keep client ASINs safe:

Step 1: Systematic Detection

- Audit all 1-3 star reviews for policy violations
- Use keyword searches within your review section
- Look for patterns across multiple negative reviews
- Document everything with screenshots and dates

Step 2: Violation Classification (these are the red flags we look for)

- Promotional content (competitor mentions, links)
- Inappropriate content (profanity, personal attacks)
- Non-product reviews (shipping, seller feedback)
- Spam/repetitive content
- Personal information exposure
- Medical claims

Step 3: Strategic Filing

- We never use Seller Support for review issues
- We submit to community-help@amazon .com and other departments with proper documentation
- Include ASIN, review screenshot, specific guideline violation
- Reference exact Amazon policy being violated

Step 4: Persistence Protocol (sometimes it takes more than one try)

- Resubmit with additional evidence and clarification
- Use different violation angles if the first approach fails
- Track submission dates and responses

Step 5: Pattern Documentation (to keep clients safe in the future)

- Identify suspicious reviewer behavior across multiple products
- Document account history showing manipulation patterns
- Present evidence of coordinated attacks when applicable

Every seller on the platform is vulnerable to TOS violating reviews...

And until you have a process like this in place, you are not protected.

I'd bet my bottom dollar this review was written by a competitor.

Sellers, here's how you should approach coordinated review attacks:

Imagine you're selling sweatshirts in a brick-and-mortar store.

The guy next door sells the same thing. 

One month before Christmas, "someone" kicks in your door and steals all your inventory.

That's exactly what happens to Amazon sellers before Prime Day.

Competitors launch a coordinated review attack right before your biggest sales period. 

One fraudulent 1-star review can drop you from 4.3 to 4.2, costing you 10-50% of your sales.

By the time you realize what's happened, the damage is already done.

Smart sellers prevent it from happening.

We built monitoring into our system because reactive doesn't work on Amazon. 

When a review hits your listing, our AI immediately checks if it violates Amazon's guidelines.

If it does, we file the case ASAP.

Timing matters on Amazon.

The longer a fraudulent review stays live, the more "helpful" votes it gets. 

Once people mark it helpful, Amazon assumes it's legitimate, even if it contains profanity or mentions competitors.

Plus, fresh attacks are easier to remove than established ones.

Protection is ALWAYS easier and lest costly than recovery.

Don't wait for the attack to plan your defense.

After removing 13,000+ reviews, I can tell you that many seemingly legitimate reviews actually violate Amazon's guidelines.

Here are some violations hiding in plain sight:

→ FBA shipping complaints ("arrived late," "damaged packaging") 
→ Pricing mentions ("paid $45 last year, now it's $58") 
→ Competitor comparisons (even in positive reviews) 
→ Any profanity (including mild words) 
→ Non-English content on US platform

Even a few of these TOS violating reviews create real damage:

When you drop from 4.3 to 4.2 stars, you lose that psychological half-star advantage.

We've seen clients lose 50% of sales from this single drop.

Your BSR tanks. PPC costs increase. Conversion rates plummet.

To protect your business, you need to understand Amazon's actual guidelines and file cases with surgical precision.

Content:
Is this review a violation of TOS?

Amazon guidelines only support reviews written in the supported language of the particular Amazon site it's written in.

So on Amazon .com (US), reviews can be in English or Spanish.

On Amazon .ca (Canada), reviews can be in English or French (and so on).

But Gen Alfa slang? That shouldn't belong anywhere, lol.

Posted Ago: 8 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
My friend opened a restaurant in Sacramento. The grand opening was packed. Everyone loved the food.

Next day, someone contacted him:

"I got sick at your event. I want $500 in gift cards."

Which in and of itself is odd. If a restaurant made me sick... why would I want a gift card to go back?

My friend offered $50.

"Not enough. Give me $500 or I'm going to hit you hard with this negative review" 

As a brand-new restaurant, this was a real threat. Their reputation was just getting off the ground. That review would have carried some heavy weight.

But instead, my friend hit them with the "Uno reverse card." He went to the local newspaper instead.

The story got picked up locally. People started talking about it.

The "review blackmail" attempt backfired.

There are a few lessons here about review psychology:

1️⃣ People don't just review products. They review experiences.

2️⃣ One bad review can erase 20 good ones in people's minds.

3️⃣ Some reviewers are weaponizing their influence.

4️⃣ The owner's response matters more than the original review.

When responding to a situation like this, you need to take emotion out of your response.

→ Acknowledge the concern 
→ Offer a solution privately
 → Don't get defensive publicly 
→ Let crazy people expose themselves

Bad response: "This customer is lying and trying to scam us!"

Good response: "We're sorry you had this experience. We'd love to make it right. Please email us directly so we can resolve this."

Other customers read your response and think: "This owner cares about fixing problems."

Not: "This owner attacks unhappy customers."

Reviews shape the public conversations about your brand.

Control the narrative by controlling your response.

How do you handle negative reviews in your business?

Content:
$3 to make, $50 to sell, infinite repeat customers = war zone.

The supplement industry is competitive. And that competition can get downright dirty.

Out of the 500 clients we monitor and manage Amazon reviews for, 9 out of our top 10 clients are in the supplement space.

The economics make review attacks profitable:

Supplements vs Other Products:

- Bike brake: $500 cost, $100 shipping to fake buyer
- Supplement: $3 cost, $5 shipping

Customer Value:

- Buy a bike brake once every 3 years
- Buy supplements every 30 days for years

Your creatine costs you $3 to make. You sell it for $50. 

If I get hooked on your supplement, I'm buying it every 30 days. 

The lifetime value could be a few thousand dollars.

So if I had ZERO morals? I have a HUGE incentive to attack my competitors with fake reviews... and the cash flow to pay for massive amounts of fake reviews.

Compare this to attacking someone selling bike parts. 

You'd spend $600 per fake attack, hoping customers see your review years later. 

With supplements, spending $25 per attack makes mathematical sense when competitors have 500+ monthly customers.

Stealing one customer means stealing hundreds of future purchases.

Content:
Revealing one of the longest-running scams in eCom:

My mom called me once: 'I just got three packages today and I didn't order anything.'"

Seems like a nice little surprise... free products... 

But here's what's happening behind the scenes:

- Competitors buy multiple units of your product
- Ship them to random addresses worldwide
- Creates "verified buyer" status
- Leave devastating one-star reviews
- Use same accounts to give themselves five-stars

Sellers: look out for these red flags: 

→ Same buyer purchasing 5+ units after "hating" the first one 
→ Exact same reviews word-for-word from different "buyers"
→ Buyers in different cities writing identical complaints

What are the odds you buy a product MULTIPLE times after you hate it? Zero. 

Nobody buys five of the same products after they hate it.

Amazon takes this seriously when you build a proper case. Build evidence that these reviews are a STATISTICAL anomaly... rather than just saying they're "mean and unfair." 

Our team has removed over 13,000 fraudulent reviews, many of them originating from this scam.

If your review velocity suddenly spikes from 1/day to 20/day, you're likely under coordinated attack. 

Document everything and build your case with data, not feelings.

When you run out of stock on Amazon, you don't just lose sales for those days.

You lose your ranking. 

You lose your momentum. 

And you lose the algorithmic equity you've spent months building.

Amazon's A9 algorithm actively penalizes products that go out of stock...it's not just pausing your position until you return.

This is how it works: 

When you stockout, Amazon redistributes your ranking equity to competitors who remained in stock. 

When you finally restock, you're essentially starting over.

The recovery time? In my experience, it typically takes 2-3x longer to regain your position than the actual stockout period lasted.

I've seen sellers who spent six months carefully building momentum, only to have a 10-day stockout set them back to near zero.

This is why inventory management is a critical marketing function.

The best sellers I work with have developed forecasting systems that account for:

- Seasonal demand fluctuations
- Amazon's warehouse receiving delays (which follow predictable patterns)
- Cash flow limitations
- Storage fee optimization

When others are frantically recovering from stockouts, these sellers maintain consistent availability...

Capturing sales and building algorithmic favor month after month.

It creates a compounding advantage that's nearly impossible to overcome.

Content:
Let me tell you what ACTUALLY gets reviews removed from Amazon (because 90% of sellers are doing it wrong).

I've personally overseen the removal of 13,000+ Amazon reviews, and there's a science to this:

Amazon doesn't care if a review is "unfair". They didn't become the #1 DTC platform in human history by making "subjective" decisions.

Does the review violate the guidelines: yes or no? THAT's the objective calculus they operate with.

These violations get results:

- Profanity/offensive content (even mild)
- Competitor mentions (direct or indirect)
- Price complaints ("too expensive")
- Shipping/seller performance comments on product reviews
- References to Amazon's own policies
- Product comparisons that name other ASINs

The problem? Most sellers flag reviews with "this isn't true" or "customer is lying." Amazon is inundated with these claims from sellers, and they take FAR more time to investigate.

So to get the best result, you need to identify the specific guideline violation, reference the exact policy, and file with the correct department.

Amazon acts as a referee. If you can't cite where the rules are being broken...the negative reviews will stay up.

Content:
"I need to find this customer and make it right - how do I get their order ID from the review?"

The tough news? You can't.

Amazon deliberately prevents sellers from connecting negative reviews to specific order IDs. It's by design.

This privacy wall protects reviewers from potential harassment but leaves legitimate sellers with limited options for resolving customer issues.

But this actually opens up a BETTER opportunity for sellers:

Instead of wasting hours hunting through your order database trying to match names (which often don't match anyway), use Amazon's comment feature to respond publicly to the review.

This strategy accomplishes two critical things:

1. It shows the unhappy customer you're responsive and willing to make things right 
2. It demonstrates to future shoppers that you stand publicly behind your products

My top-performing clients follow these review response rules:

- Reply within 24 hours - speed matters
- Keep it professional, never defensive
- Offer to resolve privately (include contact info)
- Address specific issues mentioned
- End with an action step

Remember, you're not just talking to that one unhappy customer...

You're speaking to the hundreds of potential customers who'll read it before making a purchase decision.

Your response can turn that negative into a powerful demonstration of your customer service.

Let me bust the 5 biggest myths that are holding sellers back:

Myth #1: More traffic = more sales

Not necessarily. I've seen sellers drive massive traffic but convert at 1%.

Focus on quality traffic that converts instead of raw numbers. 

One targeted buyer beats 100 random visitors every time.

Myth #2: You need thousands of products to succeed

There are plenty of 8-figure sellers with less than 10 SKUs.

You COULD throw everything at the wall to see what "sticks"... or can just find the right products with healthy margins and optimize them properly.

Myth #3: PPC is too expensive to be profitable

When your star rating drops, yes... Amazon makes you pay premium rates.

But with proper listing optimization and a solid review profile, your quality scores improve and PPC costs drop dramatically.

You can cut your PPC spend significantly just by cleaning up your review profile.

Myth #4: Competitors attacking your listings is "just part of the game"

No, it's not. And you don't have to accept it.

We see this all the time—competitors buy 5 of your products, leave 1-star reviews, then buy their own products and leave 5-stars.

This isn't "the Wild West"... it's review manipulation that violates Amazon's guidelines.

Myth #5: You can't remove negative reviews, even when they violate guidelines

This is the biggest myth of all.

We've removed over 13,000 negative reviews for clients that violated Amazon's guidelines.

These are reviews mentioning competitors, using profanity, discussing pricing, or coming from obvious fake buyer accounts.

Amazon has guidelines for a reason. If reviews violate them, they can and should be removed.

Don't let these myths hold your business back. The Amazon marketplace is challenging enough without operating on false information.

What other Amazon myths have you encountered?

Content:
A friend of mine's eye doctor has 686 Google reviews with a 4.9 rating.

As a perfectionist... being "so close yet so far" to 5 stars irked him.

But that a 4.9 rating, in ANY business/industry, is better than a 5.

Studies show people will literally question a business with 1,000 reviews and a perfect rating. 

They start thinking, "That can't be right."

Having some negative reviews shows you're human. It's going to happen anyway.

Now, if you just opened and have 3-5 reviews at 5 stars? Great. Even 10-15 reviews? Sure.

But a decade in business, seeing thousands of people, with hundreds of reviews? 4.9 is actually MORE trustworthy than 5.0.

And the good news? When you have 600+ good reviews, if someone does slam you with a 1-star, it probably won't even knock down your 4.9.

That's what reputation management is about... getting ahead of the game so one bad experience doesn't tank your entire rating.

The coveted 5-star rating is elusive and probably impossible... unless your business exists to give away perfect products, for free, with an under 5-second delivery time. 

Anything less and some busybody will find plenty of reasons to dock you stars.

What you need is a reputation strong enough to weather the occasional storm.

And in that case, being slightly imperfect is your biggest advantage.

Posted Ago: 9 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

Content:
There is an underlying trait that defines ALL revolutionary products... This Amazon review reveals what it is:

Guy uses rubber chickens to protect his car from the back of his garage. 

It's as hilarious as it is genius.

As entrepreneurs, our job is to put our product in people's hands.

What happens next? That's up to them.

Truly revolutionary products have clear and obvious benefits, while ALSO being malleable to the specific needs of each particular user.

And the BEST marketing stories of the last century reflect this:

Play-Doh was a wallpaper cleaner in the 1930s... until teachers discovered kids loved to mold and play with it.

YouTube was supposed to be a DATING SITE of all things (complete with the slogan: "tune in, hook up")... but people started uploading all kinds of videos... not just dating intros.

Kleenex was a disposable filter for gas masks during World War I, later marketed for removing makeup. But then customers said they were using it to blow their noses... the rest is history.

WD-40 was created to prevent rust on rockets (Water Displacement - 40th formula). Workers started using it at home to stop squeaky hinges and loosen stuck parts... so it was later marketed for household use.

I could go on. Forever.

So Amazon sellers: read through your reviews weekly (if not daily).

Sure, you want to be looking out for the negative/critical reviews, finding ways that you need to improve.

But you also need to CAREFULLY read the positive reviews, too.

It's the PUREST insight into what your customers actually need... and will expand the POTENTIAL of your product.

You will discover new use cases, unique value props, and authentic slogans/headlines that your copywriter would NEVER think of.

And if you embrace the creative ingenuity of your customer... working with their needs, rather than against them.

Together, you will build something revolutionary.

- Potential customers see you're attentive and responsive

Content:
Here are the 5 BIGGEST myths about Amazon reviews:

1️⃣ "It's impossible to remove negative reviews" 

We've removed over 13,000 reviews for 500+ clients. 

It's difficult but definitely possible with the right system and approach.

2️⃣ "Filing TOS Violations with Amazon doesn't do anything" 

Amazon has a great review team.

The problem? The platform is huge... and they are inundated with thousands of reports every day.

This is why the standard "click report and wait" approach has a success rate close to zero.

3️⃣ "All reviews (good and bad) are weighted equally" 

Not true. A single one-star review can outweigh 5-10 five-star reviews in Amazon's algorithm. 

People have a "negative disposition." Those critical reviews stand out like a sore thumb.

4️⃣ "Review removal is a black hat practice" 

We only target reviews that violate Amazon's own guidelines. 

It's completely compliant (we're just helping enforce the rules Amazon already has).

5️⃣ "Fight fire with fake positive reviews" 

This is the fastest way to get your account suspended. Never, ever do this.

A lot of sellers come to us saying, "We tried everything but nothing worked"...

Only to find they were using outdated approaches guided by these myths.
